stories.title: JLL Nabs Listing for 345-Unit Uptown Dallas Apartment Tower
stories.excerpt: Hines has placed an Uptown apartment development up for sale. The 22-story, 345-unit Maple Terrace Residences is being marketed by JLL. The Dallas Business Journal reports the property at 3003 Maple Ave. is part of a mixed-use property that includes 155,000 square feet of office space and more than 15,000 square feet of dining space. They are not included in the listing. Hines in 2020 purchased the historic Maple Terrace property. Construction began in 2021 to convert the roughly 100-year-old existing building into offices, while the new Maple Terrace Residences was built behind. The apartment tower opened in 2024. Maple Terrace Residences has a 95.6% lease rate and more than 28,000 square feet of amenities. The building’s nine penthouses are 100% occupied, with an average rent of $15,145. Maple Terrace Residences is located in Uptown, which is seeing substantial growth from the city’s booming “Y’all Street” financial district. The post JLL Nabs Listing for 345-Unit Uptown Dallas Apartment Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hines selling 345-unit Maple Terrace Residences in Dallas Uptown, 95.6% leased, penthouses averaging $15,145/month.
Mixed-use tower opened 2024 with 155k SF office, 15k SF dining; located in 'Y'all Street' financial district growth corridor.
New construction delivery in strongest Dallas submarket signals developer confidence but may pressure future rent growth as supply enters market.
stories.summary: Hines is marketing Maple Terrace Residences, a newly delivered 345-unit luxury apartment tower in Dallas Uptown, for sale. The 22-story building achieved 95.6% occupancy with penthouse rents averaging $15,145/month and includes 28k SF of amenities. The property is part of a mixed-use redevelopment of a historic building and is positioned in Uptown's booming 'Y'all Street' financial district.
stories.impact: A Class-A new delivery finding a buyer quickly in Uptown Dallas—despite strong absorption and rents—may indicate seller confidence in exit timing rather than fundamental demand weakness. However, increasing supply in the market's strongest submarket could moderate rent growth and compress cap rates for future acquisitions in the Uptown micromarket, particularly for Class-A product.

stories.title: NFL’s Cowboys Owner Sells Dallas Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Dallas Cowboys owner Jerry Jones’ real estate company sold one of its towers. The 313,417-square-foot office tower at 17 Cowboys Way was purchased by Hobbs Brook Real Estate, the real estate investment arm of FM Investment. The Dallas Business Journal reports the 11-story building, owned by Blue Star, is currently 98% leased to a diverse group of tenants, including Fifth Third Bank, Cain Watters & Associates, McAfee and Baker Tilly US. On-site amenities include a fitness center, conference facilities and outdoor gathering spaces. It is close to The Star’s Omni Frisco Hotel, curated dining and retail offerings, and the private Cowboys Club. Blue Star and Lincoln Property Company had partnered on the tower’s development. The 91 acres of The Star are anchored by the Dallas Cowboys’ world headquarters and practice facility. Fifth Third will later establish its regional headquarters in a new 12-story tower in Preston Center, likely in 2028. The bank intends to maintain its current office space at The Star and in southern Dallas. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post NFL’s Cowboys Owner Sells Dallas Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Jerry Jones' Blue Star sold 313k SF office tower at The Star (Frisco) to FM Investment affiliate Hobbs Brook; 98% leased.
Major tenant Fifth Third Bank will relocate regional HQ to Preston Center in 2028, though maintaining current office space.
Disposal of stabilized office asset signals potential capital redeployment away from Class-A office into higher-yielding sectors.
stories.summary: Blue Star, controlled by Jerry Jones, sold its 313,417-square-foot office tower at The Star in Frisco to Hobbs Brook Real Estate for an undisclosed price. The 11-story building is 98% leased to diverse tenants including Fifth Third Bank, which plans to establish a regional headquarters in a new Preston Center tower by 2028 while maintaining space at The Star. The asset includes significant on-site amenities and sits within The Star's 91-acre campus.
stories.impact: Office tower disposal at The Star, even though stabilized and well-leased, reflects continued capital rotation out of office into other asset classes. The retention by Fifth Third of current space despite new HQ location signals mixed signals on Frisco office fundamentals, but the deal itself is immaterial to multifamily fundamentals in the Dallas metro.

stories.title: Blackstone Reports Record AUM, Strong Earnings Powered by Data Centers, AI
stories.excerpt: Blackstone’s growth continued unabated in the first half of 2026, as the firm leveraged its enormous data center and AI platform to report strong shareholder returns. In the second quarter, the firm delivered more than 20 percent year-over-year growth across total revenues, fee revenues, fee-related earnings, net realization and distributable earnings, according to Michael Chae, vice chairman and chief financial officer. “The firm’s continued evolution and the expanding scope of our activity have fundamentally transformed our earnings power, both in terms of the magnitude and breadth and sources of earnings,” said Chae. The private equity powerhouse reported that its distributable earnings increased 26 percent year-over-year to $2 billion, with fee-related earnings growing 22 percent to $1.7 billion, or $1.29 per share, and net realizations rising by 27 percent, all despite geopolitical headwinds. Capital inflows into Blackstone reached $70 billion in the second quarter, and now stand at $260 billion in the last 12 months alone, increasing the firm’s total assets under management (AUM) 11 percent year-over-year to a firm-record $1.35 trillion. “Blackstone reported outstanding second-quarter results,” said Blackstone CEO Stephen Schwarzman. “The most significant driver of these strong results continues to be the large-scale investments we’ve made in artificial intelligence-related areas, including data centers, energy and power, and the frontier AI companies themselves.” Schwarzman noted that Blackstone has become one of the biggest private capital providers in the entire AI ecosystem, largely by building what he called “the largest data center development business in the world.” That growth has come through the firm’s 2021 acquisition of QTS Data Centers, and its direct investments into Anthropic, OpenAI, Google and SpaceX. Schwarzman also pointed out that in the second quarter Blackstone launched Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust (known by its stock symbol BX
bullets: Blackstone reports 26% YoY distributable earnings growth to $2B, 20%+ revenue growth; data centers and AI driving returns.
Capital inflows reached $70B in Q2 2026; hyperscaler infrastructure capex projected $600B+ in 2026 alone.
Record capital formation and deployment in data center/AI sectors may compete for institutional investment dollars otherwise available for multifamily development financing.
stories.summary: Blackstone delivered strong shareholder returns in H1 2026, posting 26% year-over-year growth in distributable earnings to $2 billion, powered largely by data center and AI investments. The firm reported $70 billion in capital inflows and highlighted the top five hyperscalers' projected $600+ billion infrastructure spend in 2026 alone. Fee-related earnings grew 22% to $1.7 billion, with net realizations up 27% despite geopolitical headwinds.
stories.impact: Blackstone's outsized returns in data centers and AI are likely to deepen institutional capital concentration in those sectors, tightening equity availability and raising cost-of-capital for multifamily development financing. Megafunds rotating away from residential to infrastructure may soften bids for multifamily portfolios and make new development equity harder to source at attractive returns across major metros.

stories.title: Texas People & Companies, July 24, 2026
stories.excerpt: Matthew Seliger has joined Avison Young as Principal and Market Leader for its Houston office. In this role, Seliger will lead the market’s strategic growth initiatives, expanding the firm’s capabilities across brokerage, property management, project management, and valuation services while recruiting top industry talent to support continued growth. A seasoned commercial real estate leader, Seliger brings extensive experience in agency leasing, tenant representation, strategic lease negotiations, and asset positioning. JLL Capital Markets has secured the financing of Cornerstone Plaza, a fully leased retail center located in Southlake, Texas. JLL represented the borrower, Brownlee Waggoner Holdings. The lender was Hillcrest Bank. The retail center was originally built in 2002 and comprises 51,664 square feet of net rentable area across two buildings situated on 5.9 acres with 273 parking spaces. The property is 100% leased to eight tenants and features a 9.7-year weighted average lease term.  Holt Lunsford Commercial Investments (HLCI) acquired a 26.5-acre site in North Fort Worth, Texas, for the development of Meacham 820 Crossing, a two-building Class-A industrial park totaling about 238,000 square feet. Building 1 will total approximately 105,000 square feet, and Building 2 will total approximately 133,000 square feet. Construction is expected to begin in Q4 2026, with delivery targeted for Q4 2027. Houston-based SparrowHawk added two executives to its senior leadership team. Scott Brast has joined the firm as Senior Vice President of Investments and Capital Markets, and Peter Wismer has been named Senior Vice President of Finance and Financial Reporting. Earlier this year, the firm secured a strategic partnership with Almanac Realty Investors, providing up to $300 million of growth capital to support acquisitions, portfolio expansion, and platform growth. The post Texas People & Companies, July 24, 2026 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Avison Young expands Houston office with Matthew Seliger as Principal and Market Leader; full-service capability build.
HLCI acquiring 26.5-acre North Fort Worth site for 238k SF Class-A industrial (Meacham 820 Crossing); construction Q4 2026, delivery Q4 2027.
Industrial pipeline activity in DFW remains robust; multifamily competitor for capital and development resources in same metros.
stories.summary: Avison Young hired Matthew Seliger as Principal and Market Leader for its Houston office to drive growth in brokerage, property management, and leasing. JLL Capital Markets financed a 100% leased 51.6k SF retail center in Southlake for Brownlee Waggoner Holdings. HLCI broke ground on a 238k SF, two-building Class-A industrial park (Meacham 820 Crossing) on 26.5 acres in North Fort Worth; construction starts Q4 2026 with delivery Q4 2027.
stories.impact: Continued robust industrial development pipeline in DFW competes for limited contractor labor, materials, and capital alongside multifamily. North Fort Worth industrial growth reflects broader supply-chain and logistics demand; multifamily developers should monitor labor costs and material supply tightness in the region as industrial ramps through 2027.

stories.title: Diversified Helming San Tan Valley Development
stories.excerpt: Diversified Partners is advancing a new 24-acre mixed-use development at the southeast corner of Ironwood Road and Ranch Road in San Tan Valley. The project includes approximately 25,000 square feet of proposed retail space and an approximately 50,000-square-foot medical building, which is under contract with OrthoArizona. Additional opportunities remain available for retail, restaurant, ground lease and build-to-suit users. Planned features include multiple shop buildings, a hard corner pad with drive-thru potential, two additional pad sites and a medical building. Located along Ironwood Road, it’s positioned within one of Arizona’s most active growth corridors and the nation’s second-fastest-growing county (Pinal). Diversified’s Walt Brown Jr. added, “The surrounding community continues to experience tremendous residential growth, and we’re creating a destination that meets the increasing demand for high-quality retail, dining and healthcare services while creating an exceptional long-term opportunity for businesses looking to grow alongside the surrounding community.” Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Diversified Helming San Tan Valley Development appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Diversified Partners developing 24-acre mixed-use at Ironwood Road & Ranch Road, San Tan Valley, Arizona; 25k SF retail, 50k SF medical (OrthoArizona anchor).
Pinal County is nation's second-fastest-growing county; San Tan Valley positioned in one of Arizona's most active growth corridors.
Retail/medical/mixed-use development signals strong population and household formation demand; indicates multifamily absorption headroom but retail supply may compete for rent growth.
stories.summary: Diversified Partners is advancing a 24-acre mixed-use development in San Tan Valley, Arizona, anchored by a 50k-square-foot medical building under contract with OrthoArizona and including 25k SF of retail/restaurant space. The project is positioned in Pinal County, the nation's second-fastest-growing county, within one of Arizona's most active growth corridors. Multiple pad sites and build-to-suit opportunities remain available.
stories.impact: Pinal County's #2 national growth ranking and continued retail/medical development signal robust population in-migration and household formation—strong tailwinds for multifamily demand in the San Tan Valley and greater Phoenix metro. Developers should prioritize land acquisition and entitlements in the Pinal County growth corridor before zoning and infrastructure costs escalate; absorption rates for multifamily will likely outpace retail as households move into the submarket.

stories.title: Durst Secures Three Leases at 1133 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: The Durst Organization has announced three leases totaling 11,223 square feet for prebuilt office suites at the company’s 1133 Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. In the largest of these, software company Quantexa signed a new lease for 5,263 square feet at the building. The U.K.-based firm specializes in artificial intelligence-based software applications for analysis and decision-making, according to the company website. In addition, theater company Olympus Theatricals renewed its lease for 3,345 square feet, and financial services firm Global Securitization Services renewed for 2,615 square feet. The lengths of the three leases were unclear. The Durst Organization was represented in-house by Tom Bow, Rocco Romeo and Nora Caliban. Quantexa was represented by Aidan Campbell of Colliers. “These three leases reflect what tenants are looking for right now: quality space in a best-in-class building,” said Jody Durst, president of the Durst Organization. “We are grateful that Olympus Theatricals and Global Securitization Services have recommitted to 1133 Avenue of the Americas, and welcome Quantexa to the building.” 1133 Avenue of the Americas is a 1.1 million-square-foot, 45-story Class A tower a block north of Bryant Park. Global banking and financial services firm ING Group renewed its 132,400-square-foot space at the building in December 2025, adding 21,515 square feet more for a total of 153,915 square feet throughout the seventh through 11th floors, as reported in CO. Law firm Massumi + Consoli also moved into 22,915 square feet there with a seven-year lease in August 2025. The move was an expansion for the firm, which Durst said now had five times more space than it had in its previous home at Penn 1. The month before, another law firm, Steptoe, signed a 15-year lease at 1133 Avenue of the Americas for 57,988 square feet on the building’s 43rd, 44th and 45th floors. The firm had previously been headquartered at 1114 Sixth Avenue. As of July 2
bullets: Durst Organization leased 11.2k SF across three tenants at 1133 Avenue of the Americas (Midtown South), including AI firm Quantexa (5.2k SF at $85/SF asking).
Quantexa expanding office footprint in Flatiron; theater and financial services firms renewed leases, signaling stable mixed-use tenant base.
Continued AI/tech tenant demand in Midtown South office; immaterial to multifamily but signals sustained white-collar job demand in Manhattan.
stories.summary: The Durst Organization announced three leases totaling 11,223 square feet at 1133 Avenue of the Americas, including AI software firm Quantexa signing for 5,263 SF, theater company Olympus Theatricals renewing 3,345 SF, and Global Securitization Services renewing 2,615 SF. The leasing reflects demand for quality office space in best-in-class buildings, driven in part by growing AI and tech firms seeking Midtown South locations.
stories.impact: Continued AI/tech office leasing in Midtown South is immaterial to multifamily fundamentals; however, sustained Manhattan office demand from high-wage tech tenants supports rental demand in nearby residential micromarkets. Multifamily developers with Manhattan projects should monitor tech talent influx and job growth as proxy for renter household formation and rent-growth stability.

stories.title: Formation, Crescent Teaming Up on Phase II of 689K-SF Goodyear Warehouse Facility
stories.excerpt: A venture between Formation Interests and Crescent Real Estate has broken ground on Phase II of Formation Park 10, a 261,168-square-foot industrial development in Goodyear, Arizona. The three-building expansion follows the completion of Phase I and will bring Formation Park 10 to nearly 689,000 square feet of industrial space across five buildings on 44 acres. The Project is located at the intersection of Bullard Avenue and Celebrate Life Way, with direct access and visibility to Interstate 10. Phase II is uniquely designed and the only project in West Phoenix to accommodate occupiers seeking 100,000 square feet (or more) of single-tenant space and those requiring flexible space from 15,000 to 25,000 square feet. Phase II is designed by Deutsch Architecture Group, Kimley Horn and RVI. Willmeng is the general contractor and CBRE is overseeing leasing for the project. Construction is expected to be completed in Spring 2027. The post Formation, Crescent Teaming Up on Phase II of 689K-SF Goodyear Warehouse Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Formation Interests and Crescent Real Estate broke ground on Phase II of Formation Park 10, Goodyear, Arizona; 261k SF, three buildings on 44 acres.
Phase II completion Spring 2027 will bring total to 689k SF across five buildings; accommodates 100k+ SF single-tenants and flexible 15k–25k SF suites.
West Phoenix industrial absorption and flex-space demand strong; may signal industrial-driven employment growth and logistics hub expansion competing for labor/capital in Arizona.
stories.summary: Formation Interests and Crescent Real Estate commenced Phase II of Formation Park 10 in Goodyear, Arizona, a 261,168-square-foot, three-building industrial expansion that will bring the project to nearly 689k SF across five buildings on 44 acres. The project is positioned at the intersection of Bullard Avenue and Celebrate Life Way with I-10 access and is designed to accommodate both single-tenants seeking 100k+ SF and flexible smaller occupiers. Construction expected to complete Spring 2027.
stories.impact: Strong West Phoenix industrial pipeline growth and continued spec construction indicate robust supply-chain and logistics demand. Industrial employment growth may support population in-migration and household formation in the greater Phoenix metro, benefiting multifamily absorption; however, construction labor and materials competition could tighten costs for concurrent multifamily projects in the region.

stories.title: New York’s Temporary Data Center Moratorium Could Mean Permanent Losses
stories.excerpt: On July 14, New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed the nation’s first statewide moratorium on the building of new hyperscale data centers, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year. The purpose, according to the governor’s announcement, is to take the time to establish “a nation-leading regulatory framework that protects ratepayers, the environment, the energy grid and communities across the state.” But, at a time when data center construction has become something of a national gold rush, with states competing for the jobs and economic activity new data center activity brings, many are asking if the moratorium will cost New York dearly in terms of economic growth. According to engineering and supply chain intelligence platform Accuris, the top five hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta and Oracle — are “projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36 percent increase from 2025.” And, for another illustration of just how quickly the industry is evolving, in mid-2025, Goldman Sachs projected that hyperscaler capital expenditure from 2025 through 2027 would reach $1.15 trillion, a massive increase compared to the $477 billion spent from 2022 through 2024. But the investment bank drastically revised its own estimates barely a year later, and now projects that hyperscaler capex in 2027 alone could reach $1.1 trillion. It makes sense, then, that some states are fiercely competing for this spending and the jobs it could bring, and that when a state like New York hits pause, states with more lax regulatory frameworks gain ground on bringing those dollars and jobs home. Texas and Virginia lead the nation in both current data center capacity and the willingness to indulge in as much additional data center construction as possible. According to market intelligence company Cleanview, which tracks large hyperscale data centers with identifiable power capacity, Virginia currently has 371 operating data centers with a combined capacity of
bullets: New York Governor Hochul signed nation's first statewide hyperscale data center moratorium; pauses environmental permits up to one year as of July 14.
Top five hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, Oracle) projected to spend $600B+ on infrastructure in 2026, up 36% YoY; Goldman Sachs projects $1.15T capex 2025–2027.
NY moratorium removes state from hyperscaler competition during peak capex cycle; capital and talent flows to other states, reducing economic spillover to NYC metro rental demand.
stories.summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed a one-year moratorium on new hyperscale data center environmental permits on July 14, 2026, citing the need to establish a 'nation-leading regulatory framework.' The timing is significant: the top five hyperscalers are projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36% increase from 2025, with Goldman Sachs estimating $1.15 trillion in cumulative capex from 2025–2027. States are competing aggressively for this economic activity.
stories.impact: NY's data center moratorium removes the state from competition during peak hyperscaler capex cycle, likely redirecting investment and job creation to rival states (Virginia, Texas, other hubs). This reduces near-term white-collar employment spillover to NYC metro and may soften multifamily rental demand growth in Manhattan and surrounding submarkets. Developers should monitor permitting timelines; if moratorium extends beyond one year, competitive disadvantage for NY multifamily will deepen.

stories.title: ESRT Putting 1359 Broadway Up for Sale, Seeking $225M
stories.excerpt: Empire State Realty Trust is parting ways with its mixed-use building at 1359 Broadway and marketing it for sale, sources familiar with the move told Commercial Observer. The property is expected to fetch around $225 million, with the winning buyer owning the fee simple interest in the 486,000-square-foot, 22-story office and retail property at the corner of Broadway and 36th Street two blocks north of Herald Square. The building is a short walk from both Penn Station and Grand Central Terminal and roughly 95 percent leased to a diverse mix of tenants today, with plenty of leasing momentum. Only a few days ago, manufacturer Infinium Wall Systems signed a lease for 30,017 square feet at the property, and the second quarter of 2025, ESRT signed 38,000 square feet of leases between SLCE Architects and nonprofit Braven. As for the retail component, it’s anchored by Wolfgang’s Steakhouse and also includes Japanese restaurant Wokuni. Newmark’s Adam Spies, Josh King, Adam Doneger, Avery Silverstein, Marcela Fasulo and Doug Harmon are leading the sale, sources said, and no debt currently encumbers the property. The sale of 1359 Broadway follows lots of portfolio comings and goings for ESRT. Last month the real estate investment trust (REIT) closed on the $114 million acquisition of the land beneath 111 West 33rd Street and 1400 Broadway. Two months earlier, in April, it went under contract to sell its 26-story office building at 250 West 57th Street to Namdar Realty Group for roughly $280 million. Last, but certainly not least, in December the REIT made headlines when it bought Scholastic’s building at 555-557 Broadway in SoHo for $386 million. The same Newmark team arranged both the 250 West 57th Street and Scholastic building deals. Sources familiar with the moves said ESRT is in the process of upgrading its portfolio and recycling capital in the process. ESRT and Newmark didn’t immediately return requests for comment. Cathy Cunningham can be reached at ccunningham@commer
bullets: Empire State Realty Trust selling 1359 Broadway (486k SF, 22-story office/retail at 36th & Broadway) for ~$225M; 95% leased with recent momentum.
Mixed-use property two blocks from Herald Square, walk to Penn Station and Grand Central; strong locational fundamentals.
Major REIT office asset sale near transit hubs signals continued capital recycling out of office; may compress valuations and soften office-to-residential conversion economics.
stories.summary: Empire State Realty Trust is marketing 1359 Broadway, a 486,000-square-foot, 22-story mixed-use office and retail property at the corner of Broadway and 36th Street, for approximately $225 million. The building is 95% leased with strong recent leasing momentum (30k SF to Infinium, 38k SF in Q2 2025), and retail is anchored by Wolfgang's Steakhouse and Wokuni. The property is steps from Penn Station and Grand Central Terminal.
stories.impact: ESRT's disposal of a well-leased, transit-proximate office asset at $225M (likely low-$400s/SF cap rate) confirms continued institutional flight from office. Multifamily developers evaluating office-to-residential conversion plays in Midtown South should note softening office asset values may improve land/building acquisition costs, but conversion economics remain tight absent significant zoning/entitlement tailwinds.

stories.title: Data Platform Chalk AI Grabs 27K SF in Flatiron District
stories.excerpt: Another artificial intelligence firm is planting roots in Midtown South. Chalk AI, a data platform that provides companies with infrastructure to deliver data to AI agents, has signed a lease for 26,607 square feet at Castro Properties’ 43 West 23rd Street in the Flatiron District, according to landlord broker Okada & Company. Asking rent was $85 per square foot. The deal represents Chalk AI’s second Flatiron office location after it opened its first office at 54 West 21st Street four years ago. The AI firm’s new lease also brings the eight-story office building one block west of the landmark Flatiron Building to 100 percent leased, according to Okada, as more and more AI and tech firms look to Midtown South for their offices. “We are absolutely thrilled to welcome Chalk AI to 43 West 23rd Street and to bring the property back to 100 percent occupancy,” Okada’s Christopher Okada, who represented the landlord in the deal, said in a statement. “Our firm has seen an increase of more than 200 percent in the square-footage requirements submitted by AI tenants compared with last summer. These companies are predominantly targeting well-located, renovated Class B office buildings throughout Midtown South.” The length of the lease was not disclosed. Venture Commercial’s Jason Majlessi and Arash Sadighi represented the tenant. “43 West 23rd Street and the ownership provided the opportunity for Chalk to meaningfully expand in New York City while maintaining the flexibility high-growth AI companies need out of their real estate,” Majlessi said in a statement to Commercial Observer. Spokespeople for Chalk AI and Castro Properties did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Castro Properties, run by the Castro family, has owned 43 West 23rd Street since 1972. It’s unclear when Chalk AI will move into the property. San Francisco-based Chalk AI was founded by Marc Freed-Finnegan, Elliot Marx and Andy Moreland in 2022. Last year, the company raised a $50 mil
bullets: Chalk AI leased 26.6k SF at Castro Properties' 43 West 23rd Street (Flatiron) at $85/SF asking; brings building to 100% occupancy.
Chalk AI's second Flatiron office (opened first at 54 West 21st Street four years ago); signals continued AI/tech firm clustering in Midtown South.
AI tenant agglomeration in Flatiron sustains office demand and white-collar job gravity, supporting adjacent residential rents but does not directly drive multifamily supply or cost.
stories.summary: Chalk AI, a data platform providing AI infrastructure, signed a 26,607-square-foot lease at 43 West 23rd Street in the Flatiron District at $85/SF asking rent. This marks Chalk AI's second Flatiron location, as it opened its first office at 54 West 21st Street four years prior. The lease brings the eight-story office building to 100% occupancy and reflects continued interest among AI and tech firms in Midtown South.
stories.impact: Sustained AI/tech agglomeration in Flatiron underscores strong job growth and wage premium in Midtown South, indirectly supporting multifamily rental demand and renter household formation in nearby residential markets. However, this is an office-market story and immaterial to multifamily supply, capital costs, or construction economics.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group secured $55.4M financing for 115-unit Artisan Pointe affordable housing in South Miami-Dade (Naranja); completion January 2028.
Financing: $31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M permanent loan (Berkadia), $7M from Naranja Lakes CRA (half TIF, half loan); units at 30%, 60%, 70% AMI.

Affordable housing development with stable public/LIHTC funding; demonstrates capital availability for below-market-rate projects in South Florida, not direct signal for market-rate multifamily demand.
stories.summary: NBA Hall-of-Famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group secured a $55.4 million financing package for Artisan Pointe, a 115-unit affordable housing complex in unincorporated South Miami-Dade (Naranja). The project, under construction with completion expected January 2028, is funded via $31.5M in low-income housing tax credit equity, a $16.9M permanent loan arranged by Berkadia, and $7M from the Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (half TIF, half loan). Units are restricted to 30%, 60%, and 70% AMI households.
stories.impact: Affordable housing financing activity in South Miami-Dade is immaterial to market-rate multifamily development fundamentals; however, robust LIHTC and CRA funding indicates institutional appetite for below-market housing in the region. Developers should note that affordable-housing capital availability does not necessarily translate to market-rate multifamily demand or rent growth in South Florida.

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh mixed-use office (198k SF, five-story, Downtown Austin) submitting plans 2022, expected completion late 2028; 50% preleased without breaking ground.
Endeavor HQ anchor plus 85k SF of committed tenant space de-risks project; 21k SF retail/restaurant on ground floor; parking garage included.

Downtown Austin office pipeline reportedly 'drying up'; Endeavor activity is exception. Amazon robotics facility under negotiation in separate Endeavor-owned parcel signals corporate demand spillover.
stories.summary: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin—a 198,000-square-foot, five-story office mid-rise with 21k SF of ground-floor retail/dining and below-grade parking—is advancing after plans were submitted in March 2022. Expected completion is late 2028, with Endeavor occupying approximately 25k SF as its new headquarters and 85k SF of other tenant space already committed, representing roughly 50% preleased before construction. Reports indicate Amazon may be negotiating a separate advanced robotics manufacturing facility on an Endeavor-owned parcel in the Dog's Head area.
stories.impact: Endeavor's 50% pre-lease on a 198k SF office tower in Downtown Austin—the slowest Austin office market—demonstrates developer confidence in corporate anchor-tenant demand and suggests selective office fundamentals remain stable for large users with growth stories. Amazon's parallel robotics facility negotiations signal white-collar and advanced manufacturing job creation, supporting multifamily rental demand in Austin. However, office supply remains constrained; multifamily developers should monitor whether Amazon deal closes, as it would anchor broader economic growth and justify residential supply expansion.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital Group filed permit for two-building data center project at Plaza 500 (Alexandria/Fairfax line), Virginia; 226k+ SF Phase 1, $5M estimated cost.
Proposed substation 100 feet from Bren Mar homes sparks resident opposition; neighbors pushing county to relocate project to alternate Chantilly site (purchased by Starwood affiliate for $166.8M).
Data center infrastructure in Northern Virginia faces permitting and community headwinds; may slow project timeline and raise soft costs; competing projects in Herndon also underway.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for a two-building data center project at Plaza 500 on the Alexandria/Fairfax County line in Northern Virginia, with the first building (Plaza 500 South) planned for 226k+ SF. The project also includes a proposed electrical substation located within 100 feet of homes in the Bren Mar neighborhood, sparking resident opposition and calls to relocate the project to an alternate Chantilly site (purchased by a Starwood affiliate in spring 2025 for $166.8M). Planning commission review is expected in September.
stories.impact: Regulatory and community opposition to Starwood's Northern Virginia data center faces delays and potential cost overruns from permitting and design changes. While this does not directly impact multifamily, prolonged data center construction challenges in NoVA may reduce land-value appreciation and labor competition in the submarket, modestly easing development conditions for residential projects in Fairfax County and Alexandria over the next 12–18 months.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korea's Shinsegae Group formed luxury development fund with $500M initial investment; kicking off with Aman Seoul mixed-use (Gangnam District).
Aman brand has sold $9B in branded residences in past four years; ~20 hotels under construction globally; Aman Beverly Hills penthouse poised for $200M record sale.

International luxury residential and hotel development capital formation; immaterial to U.S. multifamily market-rate demand, but signals strong ultra-high-net-worth demand for branded residential globally.
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korea's Shinsegae Group formed a luxury development fund with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are launching with a mixed-use project in Seoul's Gangnam District featuring an Aman hotel, residences, and club, and plan to expand across Asia, the Middle East, and the United States. Aman has sold $9 billion in branded residences over the past four years and has approximately 20 hotels under construction globally, with the Aman Beverly Hills penthouse poised for a record $200M condo sale.
stories.impact: OKO/Shinsegae partnership signals sustained global appetite for ultra-luxury branded residences, but is immaterial to U.S. market-rate multifamily development fundamentals. Doronin's capital raise and international expansion reflect premium-market strength; U.S. multifamily developers should note that ultra-luxury branded residential remains a separate capital stream and does not compete directly with standard Class-A/B development for equity or institutional financing.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties formed Link Apartments REIT (non-traded, closed) by consolidating funds; $1.9B portfolio, 45 properties, 5,600+ units; includes 8 Carlisle (Manhattan).
Grubb secured $377M construction loan for 8 Carlisle (462 units, Financial District, 64-story, 789 feet); also landed $240M credit facility and $617M total financing.
REIT formation to access larger balance sheets and institutional financing; signals developer confidence in refinancing/exit climate and portfolio scale advantages in capital markets.
stories.summary: Grubb Properties formed Link Apartments REIT, a non-traded, closed REIT, by consolidating multiple funds into a single entity with approximately $1.9 billion in assets, 45 properties, and 5,600+ units. The portfolio includes 8 Carlisle, a flagship 462-unit rental tower under construction in Manhattan's Financial District. Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for 8 Carlisle (from Maxim Capital and Skylight) and a $240 million credit facility, totaling $617 million in new financing across recent transactions.
stories.impact: REIT formation by a mid-market developer signals two dynamics: (1) institutional appetite for multifamily equity and debt remains available at scale, and (2) developer refinancing/exit strategies are being priced assuming sustained capitalization and debt availability. Grubb's ability to secure $617M in fresh capital across construction and working-capital facilities suggests benign financing conditions for well-capitalized developers with stabilized portfolios, bolstering entry and development economics for peers in major metros like New York.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC permit week ending July 17: largest new building 112.3k SF mixed-use (8-story, 50 units) at Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens; Queens project 82.7k SF (10-story, 86 units) at Vernon Boulevard.
Bronx two-building apartment complex (198 units total, nine stories each) filed in Tremont section; consistent small-to-mid-sized mixed-use and residential filings.
NYC residential pipeline remains active with modest projects (50–100 unit range); no megaproject filings; reflects continued entitlement processing despite office market constraints.
stories.summary: NYC's top construction permits filed in week ending July 17 included a 112.3k-SF mixed-use project (eight stories, 50 apartments) at 103 Empire Boulevard (Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens), an 82.7k-SF, 10-story, 86-unit mixed-use building in Queens at Vernon Boulevard, and a two-building, 198-unit residential complex (nine stories each) in the Bronx's Tremont section. Other notable filings included major alterations at Brookfield's 250 Vestry Street (192.2k SF) and JPMorgan Chase's 30k-SF renovation in Downtown Brooklyn.
stories.impact: NYC residential pipeline remains steady with modest 50–100 unit projects across outer boroughs and mixed-use combinations, indicating continued zoning demand and acquisition activity despite office-market softness. Developers should note the Bronx's continued inflow of entitlements and that outer-borough multifamily absorption remains strong; however, the absence of large-format residential filings suggests constrained mega-site availability or capital concentration in stabilized assets rather than new development.
